proposals.title: Asymmetric Tabletop Interfaces: Studying Forced Role Emergence in Collaborative Problem-Solving
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 HCI research on collaborative interfaces overwhelmingly assumes equal access to information and controls is optimal, a principle rooted in CSCW and tabletop computing. However, real-world collaboration often involves inherent asymmetries in knowledge, access, or skill. We lack a principled understanding of how deliberately engineered asymmetries in shared interfaces affect communication patterns, role formation, and collective outcomes, which is critical for designing systems for education, accessibility, and specialized workflows.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that intentionally asymmetric views and controls on a shared tabletop interface will force the emergence of distinct, stable participant roles (e.g., director, executor, observer), leading to different communication strategies and problem-solving efficiencies compared to a symmetric baseline.
proposals.method: We will develop a custom multi-touch tabletop application where dyads collaborate on a visual puzzle task (e.g., tile assembly). Using a gaze-contingent architecture (inspired by Morris's prior work), we will instrument asymmetric conditions: one user has a full overview map but cannot touch, while the other has touch control but only a local view. We will log all touch, gaze (via external eye-tracker), and vocal communication. Behavior will be analyzed using sequence analysis of action-gaze patterns and conversational analysis to characterize role-driven strategies. Agent-based models will be used to simulate potential interaction patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel framework for designing and analyzing asymmetric collaborative interfaces, 2) empirical findings on how constrained access shapes implicit role assignment and communication, and 3) design implications for systems where equitable access is not possible or desirable, such as in assistive technology or training scenarios.
proposals.novelty: While asymmetry has been studied in distributed systems, applying it as a deliberate design lens to a shared physical tabletop to *force* role emergence challenges a core tenet of co-located collaborative interface design. The integration of gaze and action logging provides a fine-grained lens on the coordination mechanisms that underpin this emergence.

proposals.title: Task Modality Matters: Characterizing How Generative AI Agents and Humans Collaborate Across Analysis, Planning, and Design Tasks
proposals.motivation: Research on human-AI collaboration, particularly with generative AI, has heavily focused on document-centric tasks like writing and editing. However, real-world collaboration involves diverse activities like project planning and creative design. Understanding how task modality influences collaboration dynamics and outcomes is crucial for designing more effective, context-aware AI partners, a key challenge in HCI and CSCW pre-2026.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the effectiveness of different human-AI collaboration styles (e.g., turn-taking, delegation, mixed-initiative) will vary significantly across task types (analysis, planning, design), and that these differences can be predicted by measurable interaction signals like gaze patterns and communication latency.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled lab study where participants collaborate with a scripted LLM-based agent (e.g., using GPT-4 or a similar pre-2026 model via a structured API) on three distinct tasks: text analysis, event planning, and UI mock-up design. We will instrument the interface to log chat, edits, and timing, and use the SpeakFaster Observer framework to capture eye-gaze patterns. We will analyze collaboration using metrics of efficiency, communication entropy, and subjective preference, correlating them with task type and the captured behavioral signals.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a empirical taxonomy of human-generative AI collaboration across tasks, identifying which interaction protocols are most suitable for different goals. It will contribute a novel multi-modal dataset (language, gaze, behavior) for studying collaborative AI and offer concrete design guidelines for building task-adaptive AI collaborators.
proposals.novelty: While prior work studies AI collaboration on single tasks, this systematically varies task modality to isolate its effect. It uniquely combines generative AI agents with fine-grained gaze instrumentation—a method from AAC research—to decode implicit collaboration signals beyond dialogue.

proposals.title: VisionProfileAgent: A Generative Agent Framework for Automated Assistive Tool Configuration Based on Clinical Visual Impairment Profiles
proposals.motivation: People with visual impairments face a fragmented landscape of digital accessibility tools (e.g., screen magnifiers, high-contrast themes, screen readers). Current manual selection and configuration is burdensome, often requiring expert knowledge, leading to tool underutilization and reduced digital access. This problem is acute in communication contexts like Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC), where tool mismatch can severely hinder interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: A system that maps structured clinical vision profiles (e.g., diagnosis, acuity, field loss) to a parameterized space of tool functionalities and interface adjustments can automatically generate effective, personalized tool configurations, thereby reducing setup burden and improving functional outcomes.
proposals.method: We will develop a generative agent-based system. First, we will create a knowledge base by semantically modeling tool capabilities (from APIs and manuals) and clinical vision profiles (from ophthalmology literature). A reasoning agent, built on a pre-trained language model (e.g., GPT-4 or open-source Llama 2), will be fine-tuned to map profile features to tool parameters using a dataset of expert-validated configurations. The system's output will be executable configuration scripts or recommendations, evaluated via a user study with participants having diverse visual impairments, measuring task performance and subjective usability against a manual setup baseline.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes 1) a novel semantic model linking clinical vision concepts to computational interface parameters, 2) an operational, evaluable framework for automated, personalized accessibility setup, and 3) empirical evidence on the efficacy of AI-driven tool configuration for visual impairments, extending human-AI interaction research into clinical accessibility domains.
proposals.novelty: While prior work analyzes tool use or single impairments (e.g., gaze-typing), this integrates generative AI agents with structured clinical knowledge for *multi-tool*, *condition-specific* automation, moving beyond recommendation to executable configuration.

proposals.title: Generating Accessible Image Descriptions: A Large-Scale Evaluation of Generative AI with Diverse Low-Vision Users
proposals.motivation: Generative AI models show promise for automating image descriptions (alt-text), a critical accessibility feature. However, pre-2026 evaluations are limited in scale and participant diversity, often involving sighted users or small groups with specific visual impairments. This fails to capture the heterogeneous needs of the low-vision community, which includes conditions like macular degeneration, diabetic retinopathy, and retinitis pigmentosa with varying severity, field loss, and contrast sensitivity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the perceived utility and preference for AI-generated image descriptions will vary systematically across low-vision conditions and severities, and that fine-tuning generative models on feedback from this diverse population will produce descriptions rated as more useful than those from general-purpose models.
proposals.method: We will recruit 150+ participants with clinically characterized low-vision conditions, stratifying by type and severity (e.g., using ETDRS charts and visual field tests). Using a custom web platform, participants will evaluate descriptions for Wikipedia-style images generated by three systems: a baseline (BLIP-2), a state-of-the-art general model (GPT-4V with prompting), and a model fine-tuned on our initial feedback data. Evaluations will use Morris's established 'Slide' method for comparative rating and collect gaze data during task-based comprehension assessments. We will employ multilevel regression to analyze how condition and severity predict description preference and task performance.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, condition-specific evidence base for AI-generated alt-text utility, moving beyond 'one-size-fits-all' metrics. It will yield a novel dataset pairing images, multiple AI descriptions, and structured human evaluations from a diverse low-vision population. The findings will directly inform the design of more personalized, condition-aware generative models for accessibility.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the rigorous, large-scale disaggregation of low-vision user feedback by medical condition and severity to drive AI model design, combining clinical characterization with HCI evaluation methods and generative AI fine-tuning.

proposals.title: Plugging the Gap: A Developer-Centric Evaluation of Post-Hoc Plugin Usability and Integration Effort
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of generative AI models has led to a surge in post-hoc plugins that add capabilities like safety filters or style transfer to existing systems. However, their adoption is bottlenecked by developer experience. Without formal studies on integration effort and usability, tool creators lack evidence to improve APIs, hindering the broader, responsible deployment of generative AI in applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that current post-hoc plugin architectures introduce significant, measurable friction for developers during integration, and that this friction can be characterized and reduced through specific, evidence-based design improvements to plugin interfaces.
proposals.method: We will conduct a mixed-methods study with 20-30 developers. First, an instrumented coding task will log integration time, errors, and API usage patterns as behavioral data. Second, semi-structured interviews and validated surveys (e.g., NASA-TLX) will capture subjective usability and cognitive load. Analysis will combine quantitative metrics with thematic coding of interview transcripts to identify key pain points.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first empirical model of developer friction for post-hoc AI plugins, offering concrete design guidelines for Human-AI Interaction. It will extend HCI methods for evaluating developer tools into the generative AI space, contributing a replicable study protocol for assessing AI integration APIs.
proposals.novelty: While developer experience is studied in software engineering, applying rigorous HCI instrumentation (e.g., behavioral logging, cognitive load assessment) specifically to the novel paradigm of post-hoc generative AI plugins is unexplored. It bridges Morris's expertise in gaze/behavior instrumentation with the emerging study of generative AI tooling.

proposals.title: Gaze-Contingent Generative Slides: Enhancing Presentation Comprehension with Foveated Content Adaptation
proposals.motivation: Generative AI is rapidly being used to create presentation slides, but these slides often suffer from information overload, hindering audience comprehension. Pre-2026 vision science demonstrates that gaze-contingent displays can guide attention and reduce perceptual load, yet this technique has not been applied to the social, communicative context of slide-based presentations. This work addresses the critical gap between generative content creation and effective human communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a slide presentation system which dynamically adapts generative visual and textual content based on real-time audience gaze patterns will improve information retention and subjective comprehension compared to static slides.
proposals.method: We will develop a prototype system using a Tobii eye-tracker and a modified presentation client. The system will employ a real-time gaze analysis agent to classify the audience's aggregate foveal region. Using this input, a rule-based and neural style transfer pipeline (e.g., AdaIN) will apply perceptual enhancements: de-emphasizing non-foveal content via foveated blurring and highlighting key terms or diagram elements in the foveal region. We will evaluate this through a within-subjects study comparing our system against static slides, measuring recall and comprehension scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel framework for 'attentive generative communication,' bridging HCI, vision science, and AI. It will provide empirical evidence on how gaze-contingent principles can be used not for display efficiency, but for communicative efficacy, offering new design guidelines for generative AI tools in presentation software.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in repurposing foveated rendering—traditionally for graphics performance—as a real-time communicative signal to modulate generative content, and in evaluating this approach within the social, multi-person context of a presentation, rather than for individual VR/AR use.

proposals.title: AR-Observer: An Agent-Based Toolkit for Measuring Communication and Behavior in Augmented Reality
proposals.motivation: Augmented Reality (AR) is emerging as a platform for novel social and assistive interactions, such as real-time translation overlays or contextual aids. However, pre-2026 methods for instrumenting and analyzing communication, like the eye-gaze typing metrics in SpeakFaster Observer, are designed for 2D screens and cannot capture the complex, situated behaviors in 3D AR. There is a critical need for toolkits that can measure how AR visual enhancements affect user behavior, attention, and communication clarity in real-world physical contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by adapting 2D screen-based instrumentation paradigms to 3D AR environments using a multi-agent architecture, we can develop a robust toolkit that quantifies how AR augmentations influence user gaze, interaction patterns, and communication efficacy.
proposals.method: We will extend the SpeakFaster Observer architecture into AR (e.g., Microsoft HoloLens 2). The method involves: 1) Developing AR-native logging agents that capture 3D gaze vectors, head pose, and hand interactions relative to physical and virtual objects. 2) Implementing a spatial registration module using SLAM (Simultaneous Localization and Mapping) to anchor behavioral data to the physical environment. 3) Employing a rule-based and generative (e.g., GPT-4 API) agent system to segment interaction logs into meaningful communication episodes and analyze the alignment between augmentations and user intent, inspired by Morris's work on generative AI perspectives.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute the first open-source toolkit for longitudinal, in-situ measurement of communication behavior in AR, directly extending Morris's instrumentation research to a new modality. It will produce novel datasets and metrics for evaluating AR accessibility and design, offering empirical methods to study how generative AR content supports or hinders real-world communication.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing pre-2026 agent-based behavioral logging with 3D spatial computing, creating a methodology to quantitatively study the 'blending' of virtual enhancements with the physical world—a core, yet poorly instrumented, challenge in AR communication and design.

proposals.title: Simulating Social Dynamics with Multimodal Generative Agents: Moving Beyond Text-Only Interaction
proposals.motivation: Social simulacra, or generative agent-based simulations of human interaction, have emerged as a powerful tool for studying communication and design. However, existing systems are predominantly text-only, which fails to capture the richness of real-world social exchanges that involve prosody, gaze, gesture, and shared visual context. This limitation is significant for modeling authentic scenarios in areas like collaborative design, assistive communication (AAC), and cross-cultural interaction, where multimodal cues are critical.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting social simulacra with multimodal interaction channels (voice, synthetic video, and shared virtual spaces) will produce agent behaviors that are more socially plausible, exhibit richer communicative dynamics, and better support the simulation of complex, cooperative tasks compared to text-only baselines.
proposals.method: We will extend a framework like Generative Agents by integrating multimodal modules. For voice, we will use pre-2026 text-to-speech (TTS) models (e.g., VALL-E, TortoiseTTS) and automatic speech recognition (ASR) to enable spoken dialogue. For visual presence, we will generate agent avatars and simple gestures using latent diffusion models (e.g., Stable Diffusion) or neural radiance fields (NeRFs), informed by agent state. Shared virtual spaces will be modeled as 2D grids or 3D environments where agent 'gaze' and proximity, building on Morris's prior gaze instrumentation work, influence interaction. Agent architecture will be based on large language models (LLMs) where multimodal perceptions are encoded into the prompt context.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, open-source framework for multimodal social simulation, enabling new research into how non-textual cues shape collective behavior and communication. It will provide validated methods for measuring multimodal interaction plausibility and offer a testbed for studying the accessibility and cross-linguistic dynamics of communication technologies, extending Morris's work on image accessibility and AAC.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored multimodal AI or text-based social simulacra in isolation, this proposal uniquely integrates them to create agents that communicate through coordinated speech, visual expression, and shared environmental context, moving beyond the sequential chat paradigm that dominates the field.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Social Sandboxes: A Participatory Framework for Shaping Agent Personalities and Community Norms
proposals.motivation: Generative AI agents are increasingly deployed in social simulations and interactive systems, yet their personalities and the rules governing their communities are typically designed by researchers, risking misalignment with user values and needs. This is particularly critical for applications in communication and design, where agent behavior must be trustworthy and culturally situated. Pre-2026, methods for democratizing this design process, especially using real-time behavioral data like gaze, remain underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: That a structured participatory design process, leveraging users' implicit behavioral signals (e.g., gaze patterns during interaction) and explicit feedback, will yield agent personality parameters and community rules that are perceived as more coherent, engaging, and aligned with user expectations than those designed by experts alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a web-based sandbox environment where small groups of participants interact with a community of LLM-based agents (e.g., using GPT-4 or similar pre-2026 architectures). The method integrates Morris's prior work on gaze-instrumented interfaces (SpeakFaster Observer) and generative AI perspectives. Participants will collaboratively adjust agent personality traits (via sliders mapped to LLM parameters like temperature, system prompts) and propose community rules through iterative design sessions. Their gaze heatmaps on agent dialogue and interface elements will serve as implicit input to guide discussions and refine designs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodological framework for human-AI co-design of social simulations, documented with case studies. It will produce empirical findings on how participatory processes shape perceived agent personality and community dynamics, offering concrete design guidelines for more user-centered generative AI systems in social and communicative domains.
proposals.novelty: While participatory design is known in HCI, its application to the real-time, iterative shaping of LLM agent personalities and multi-agent community rules—using a combination of explicit feedback and implicit behavioral instrumentation like gaze—is a novel synthesis of Morris's expertise in gaze, generative AI, and communication design.

proposals.title: Generative Agent Pools: Designing and Evaluating Large-Scale Human-Agent Collaborative Communication
proposals.motivation: Generative AI agents are moving from dyadic human-computer interaction to mediating and participating in larger social groups, such as in collaborative design, online communities, and workplace teams. Pre-2026 research on generative agents focuses on small-scale, scripted interactions, leaving a critical gap in understanding how to architect systems for dozens of simultaneous human and agent participants. This work is essential for designing future collaborative platforms that are effective, equitable, and comprehensible.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a communication architecture based on pools of specialized, role-based generative agents, coupled with explicit turn-taking and state-sharing protocols, will enable more coherent and productive large-scale human-agent interaction compared to unstructured multi-agent chat.
proposals.method: We will design a text-based collaborative world-building platform where 20-50 human participants interact via a pool of 10-15 generative agents. The system will use a hierarchical agent architecture: 'mediator' agents (based on LLMs like GPT-4) will manage turn-taking and topic flow using a shared state ledger, while 'specialist' agents (fine-tuned for roles like summarizer, scribe, or domain expert) will be invoked by mediators or humans. We will instrument interaction using methods from Morris's prior work, logging gaze patterns (via webcam) on agent-generated 'slides' of group state and analyzing communication behavior and breakdowns.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel system architecture and design patterns for large human-agent groups, 2) empirical findings on communication dynamics and breakdowns in these settings, and 3) new methods for evaluating group-scale AI-mediated interaction, extending human-AI interaction research beyond dyads.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond studying single agents or small, closed groups to tackle the emergent coordination and scale problems of dozens of hybrid human-agent participants, a frontier largely unexplored in pre-2026 HCI and AI research.

proposals.title: Generating Personalized Accommodations: An Agent-Based Simulation of Neurodivergent Communication in Collaborative Tasks
proposals.motivation: Current workplace and educational accommodations for neurodivergent conditions like ASD and ADHD are often standardized, based on broad diagnostic categories rather than individual needs. This one-size-fits-all approach fails to account for the significant heterogeneity within these populations, potentially leading to ineffective support. Grounded in pre-2026 calls for personalized accessibility and the rise of generative AI for bespoke solutions, this research addresses a critical gap in designing truly supportive socio-technical systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that simulating individuals with nuanced, multi-dimensional neurodivergent profiles using generative language agents will reveal that optimal communication accommodations are highly personalized and cannot be predicted by diagnostic label alone.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent simulation framework where agents, parameterized by behavioral vectors derived from pre-2026 literature (e.g., attention span, sensory sensitivity, social reciprocity), engage in collaborative slide-design tasks. Using a generative language model (e.g., GPT-4) as the core agent architecture, we will instrument interactions to measure communication efficiency and conflict. We will systematically vary agent parameters and accommodation strategies (e.g., structured agendas, asynchronous feedback, gaze-based attention prompts) to identify which accommodations best suit which agent profiles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, generative methodology for modeling neurodivergent communication and a taxonomy of personalized accommodation strategies. It will provide empirical evidence against blanket accommodation policies and offer a simulation-based tool for designers and educators to prototype and evaluate support systems before real-world deployment.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has characterized needs or used AI for diagnosis, this proposal uniquely combines generative agent-based simulation with a fine-grained, continuous modeling of neurodivergent traits to dynamically test and generate personalized accommodations, moving beyond static surveys or clinical observations.

proposals.title: The Inclusive Team Effect: Quantifying the Impact of Communication Accommodations on Software Development Productivity and Quality
proposals.motivation: While inclusive practices like providing communication accommodations are widely advocated for in software teams, their impact is often argued anecdotally. There is a lack of empirical, quantitative evidence linking specific accommodations to objective measures of team output and software quality. This gap makes it difficult for organizations to make data-driven decisions about investing in inclusive tooling and processes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that software development teams using specific, instrumented communication accommodations (e.g., gaze-typing support, AI-assisted summarization) will show measurable improvements in productivity metrics (e.g., commit frequency, cycle time) and code quality metrics (e.g., reduced bug-fix commits, improved code review sentiment) compared to baseline periods or control groups.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, mixed-methods study within a software organization. First, we will instrument an existing communication platform (e.g., Slack, Teams) with lightweight agents that log the use of specific accommodations derived from Morris's prior work, such as real-time captioning or gaze-typing integration. We will correlate this usage data with software repository metrics (from Git) and issue tracker data (e.g., Jira). Agent-based modeling will be used to simulate team communication flows with and without accommodations to isolate their effects. Qualitative interviews will contextualize the quantitative findings.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative framework for measuring the return on investment (ROI) of inclusive communication tools in a technical context. It will contribute a novel dataset linking accommodation use to engineering outcomes and offer evidence-based guidelines for teams to adopt inclusive practices that tangibly benefit productivity.
proposals.novelty: This study moves beyond characterizing accessibility barriers to actively measuring the systemic impact of removing them. It uniquely combines instrumentation of assistive communication tools with fine-grained software engineering analytics, an approach not seen in prior HCI or software engineering literature.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Neurodiverse Workspaces: A Participatory Study on Generative AI Agents for Communication and Task Management
proposals.motivation: Neurodiverse individuals, such as those with autism or ADHD, often face significant workplace barriers related to communication, sensory overload, and executive function, leading to high attrition among talented engineers. While workplace accommodations exist, they are frequently generic and not co-designed with the employees they aim to support. This project addresses this gap by applying participatory design, a core HCI method, to develop personalized, AI-augmented interventions that are truly responsive to individual needs and work styles.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a structured participatory design process with neurodiverse engineers will yield a novel class of personalized, generative AI-based workplace tools that measurably improve self-reported communication efficacy and reduce daily task management burden compared to existing one-size-fits-all solutions.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of participatory design workshops with neurodiverse software engineers. Sessions will utilize probes like the 'SpeakFaster Observer' methodology for modeling communication patterns and 'slide'-based storyboarding to prototype interventions. Co-designed prototypes will focus on generative AI agents (e.g., for email drafting or meeting summarization, informed by Morris's work on scientists' perspectives) and ambient behavior-tracking systems (using gaze and activity data) to provide just-in-time support for focus and task transitions. The final phase involves deploying a high-fidelity prototype in a longitudinal field study to measure behavioral and subjective outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel participatory design framework specifically for neurodiverse workplace technology, 2) a set of co-designed prototype specifications for generative AI agents that support neurodivergent communication and work practices, and 3) empirical data on the efficacy and acceptance of such personalized AI interventions in a real engineering context.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in merging deep participatory design with neurodiverse stakeholders—a group often studied but less often empowered as design partners—with the technical development of generative AI agents, moving beyond generic accessibility features to create highly personalized, agentic support systems grounded in direct lived experience.

proposals.title: Platform-Specific Phrasing: A Cross-Network Analysis of Question Framing Effects on Engagement and Response Quality
proposals.motivation: Prior research on question phrasing, often conducted on platforms like Reddit or Stack Exchange, has shown that subtle linguistic changes can significantly impact engagement and answer quality. However, the social norms, user demographics, and communication purposes of platforms like Twitter (for rapid, public discourse) and LinkedIn (for professional, identity-focused discussion) are fundamentally different. Understanding how these platform-specific contexts mediate the effects of question design is crucial for developing robust, generalizable theories of online communication and for building effective generative AI tools that assist users across platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: The efficacy of different question-phrasing strategies (e.g., direct vs. open-ended, inclusion of context) on eliciting high-quality responses and engagement is not universal but is instead moderated by the social and functional norms of the platform (e.g., Twitter vs. LinkedIn).
proposals.method: We will design a controlled, longitudinal field experiment using automated agent-based posting. We will adapt a set of semantically equivalent question variants from prior studies (e.g., on Reddit) and deploy them via custom bots on Twitter (via its API) and LinkedIn (via manual protocols respecting platform ToS). We will instrument these posts to log engagement metrics (likes, replies, reposts) and will use a pre-trained language model (e.g., BERT or a fine-tuned version of GPT-3) to code reply quality along dimensions of informativeness and relevance. Statistical analysis (ANOVA, mixed-effects models) will test for interaction effects between phrasing type and platform.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first empirical comparison of generative question-design principles across major, distinct social platforms. It will yield concrete design guidelines for communicators and for AI-assisted writing tools tailored to specific online contexts. Furthermore, it will contribute a novel methodological framework for cross-platform computational social science research.
proposals.novelty: While question phrasing has been studied, prior work is largely siloed within single platforms. This study's novelty lies in its direct, controlled cross-platform experimental design, explicitly testing the boundary conditions of communication strategies and linking platform sociology to measurable interaction outcomes.

proposals.title: Gaze Patterns in the Feed: How Question Phrasing Biases Visual Attention in Algorithmically Curated News Contexts
proposals.motivation: The phrasing of questions in news headlines and social media posts (e.g., 'Is X a risk?' vs. 'How does X work?') is known to influence cognitive framing and belief. Prior to 2026, the impact of such phrasing on real-time, subconscious visual attention within complex, multi-item newsfeeds is poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to design communication that supports deliberate, rather than manipulated, information processing.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that question phrasing that implies controversy or threat (e.g., 'Is this dangerous?') will elicit faster fixations and longer dwell times on affectively charged or confirmatory elements of a news item, compared to neutral or exploratory phrasing, thereby shaping the user's attentional pathway before conscious evaluation.
proposals.method: We will instrument a simulated newsfeed interface with a Tobii Pro Spectrum eye-tracker to collect high-frequency gaze data (fixations, saccades, dwell times). Using a within-subjects design, participants will view feed items where target stories are paired with systematically varied question phrasings (manipulating valence and epistemic stance). We will employ established computational methods for scanpath comparison (e.g., Needleman-Wunsch for sequence alignment) and statistical analysis of Area of Interest (AOI) metrics using mixed-effects models to quantify phrasing effects on attention distribution.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a fine-grained, behavioral dataset linking micro-level linguistic features to macro-level visual attention patterns in a ecologically valid feed context. It will yield design principles for 'attentional nudges' that can be used to either mitigate cognitive bias or, in assistive contexts like AAC (augmentative and alternative communication) informed by Morris's prior work, to optimize information scanning.
proposals.novelty: While eye-tracking is common in psychology and HCI, its application to dissect the effects of subtle linguistic variables within dynamic, generative newsfeed environments—bridging social computing, language design, and behavioral measurement—is novel. It extends Morris's gaze-typing instrumentation to a new domain of generative content consumption.

proposals.title: Culturally-Aware Search Agents: Adapting Social Search Behavior and Interface Through Gaze and Interaction Modeling
proposals.motivation: Current search systems often assume a universal user model, potentially disadvantaging users from diverse cultural backgrounds in how information is ranked, presented, or interacted with. This is critical for social search, where relevance is co-constructed through community behavior and communication norms. Pre-2026 work in image accessibility and cross-language Wikipedia highlights systemic cultural biases in information systems that this research directly addresses.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that modeling users' implicit behavioral signals (e.g., gaze patterns during search) and explicit interaction histories can infer culturally-influenced search preferences, and that a generative agent-based simulation framework can be used to design search interfaces and algorithms that adapt to these preferences, improving perceived relevance and efficiency.
proposals.method: We will first instrument a search interface with eye-tracking (extending the SpeakFaster Observer methodology) to collect gaze and interaction data from a culturally diverse user pool completing search tasks. Using this data, we will train a user-modeling agent to classify cultural search styles (e.g., holistic vs. analytic information scanning). This agent will then drive a generative simulation where multiple agent 'personas' interact with candidate adaptive interfaces (e.g., varying result density, visual hierarchy, or social cue prominence). The most robust interface and ranking adaptations (e.g., re-weighting social signals based on inferred cultural style) will be implemented and evaluated in a controlled user study.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes: 1) a novel, behaviorally-grounded framework for inferring culturally-influenced search preferences, 2) a generative agent-based method for prototyping culturally-adaptive systems, and 3) empirical evidence on the efficacy of specific interface and algorithmic adaptations for improving cross-cultural search equity.
proposals.novelty: While prior work examines cultural bias in algorithms or static interfaces, our approach is novel in its use of real-time behavioral sensing (gaze) and generative agent simulation to dynamically *design and adapt* social search systems, bridging the gap between measurement and adaptive design.

proposals.title: From Perceptions to Patterns: Validating Generative AI's Role in Social Q&A through Large-Scale Behavioral Log Analysis
proposals.motivation: Recent survey work, such as that on scientists' perspectives on generative AI, reveals expectations about how AI might transform knowledge-seeking and sharing on social platforms. However, there is a critical gap between these self-reported perceptions and evidence of how AI tools are actually integrated into everyday social Q&A behaviors. Understanding this real-world usage is essential for designing communication systems that effectively support human-AI collaboration.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that observable behavioral patterns in social Q&A platforms (e.g., post length, response time, interaction chains) will reveal a significant, measurable influence of generative AI tools, and that these empirical patterns will systematically diverge from the perceptions captured in prior survey-based studies.
proposals.method: We will conduct a log analysis of public social Q&A data from platforms like Facebook Groups or Stack Exchange, focusing on periods surrounding the public release of major generative AI models (e.g., GPT-4). Using agent-based modeling frameworks to simulate interaction patterns, we will train classifiers (e.g., BERT-based models) to detect AI-assisted content from linguistic and structural features. We will correlate these detections with behavioral metrics such as gaze-typing-derived efficiency measures (extending the SpeakFaster Observer methodology) and cross-lingual information flow, adapting methods from prior work on image accessibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, behavioral validation of generative AI's integration into social knowledge work, moving beyond speculative surveys. It will contribute a novel methodological framework for distinguishing AI-mediated human communication and offer concrete design implications for social platforms and assistive technologies aiming to leverage generative AI.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in fusing Morris's expertise in instrumentation (gaze-typing) and generative AI surveys with a robust, agent-informed log analysis to empirically ground a highly discussed but poorly measured social phenomenon, creating a new lens on human-AI communication design.

proposals.title: Turn-Taking and Repair in Asynchronous Q&A: A Computational Conversation Analysis of Online Communities
proposals.motivation: Online Q&A platforms (e.g., Stack Exchange, Reddit) are critical for knowledge sharing, but their asynchronous, text-based nature creates unique communicative challenges. Pre-2026 research on computer-mediated communication often lacks fine-grained linguistic analysis of how successful and unsuccessful help-seeking interactions are structured. Understanding these patterns is essential for designing better AI assistants and community moderation tools that support effective, accessible communication.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that successful Q&A threads exhibit distinct, quantifiable patterns in conversational structure—specifically in turn-taking adjacency pairs and repair sequences—compared to threads that fail to produce satisfactory answers. These patterns will correlate with user engagement metrics and perceived answer quality.
proposals.method: We will apply computational conversation analysis (CA) to large-scale Q&A corpora. Using pre-2026 NLP toolkits (e.g., spaCy, NLTK) and rule-based classifiers, we will automatically code for CA features: adjacency pairs (question-answer, assessment-elaboration), initiation-response-evaluation sequences, and repair mechanisms (e.g., clarification requests, reformulations). We will train agent-based models to simulate interaction patterns and validate findings by integrating behavioral metrics (e.g., gaze patterns on re-typed questions from the 'SpeakFaster Observer' methodology) and thread longevity.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel computational framework for applying conversation analysis to asynchronous social media data. It will produce a taxonomy of interactional success/failure in Q&A, offering empirical insights for the design of generative AI that can participate more naturally in community discussions and for improving the accessibility of information exchange across languages and user groups.
proposals.novelty: While CA is well-established for synchronous talk, its systematic computational application to large-scale, asynchronous Q&A is novel. The integration of linguistic analysis with behavioral instrumentation (gaze-typing) and generative AI agent modeling to validate interaction patterns moves beyond purely text-centric analyses common pre-2026.

proposals.title: The Substitution Effect: Modeling How Generative AI and Social Media Engagement Influences Offline Support-Seeking Behavior
proposals.motivation: Prior to 2026, there is growing concern that generative AI and social platforms are becoming primary sources for informational and emotional support, particularly for sensitive topics like mental health. This shift may create a 'digital comfort zone,' reducing motivation to seek more structured, in-person services that could be more effective or crucial for long-term well-being. Understanding this potential substitution effect is critical for designing communication systems that augment, rather than inadvertently replace, essential human services.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that individuals who heavily engage with generative AI agents and social networks for support on sensitive topics will demonstrate a measurable decrease in their likelihood to pursue referrals to relevant offline, professional services over time.
proposals.method: We will instrument a longitudinal, agent-based simulation environment where participant avatars, parameterized by real-world behavioral and gaze-typing data (extending the SpeakFaster Observer methodology), navigate support-seeking scenarios. A generative AI language model (e.g., a fine-tuned GPT-4 variant) will act as the social/media environment, responding to queries. We will track simulated 'decision slides'—moments where the agent can accept an AI-suggested offline service referral—using a reinforcement learning framework to model the decay in referral acceptance probability as a function of prior AI engagement depth and satisfaction.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel computational model of the support-seeking substitution effect, grounded in real user interaction patterns. It will provide the HCI and social computing communities with a method to proactively evaluate the offline behavioral impacts of generative AI systems, moving beyond usability to assess broader societal consequences.
proposals.novelty: While prior work characterizes online accessibility or scientists' perspectives on AI, this study uniquely combines agent-based modeling, longitudinal behavioral instrumentation (gaze-typing), and generative AI to simulate and quantify a causal relationship between online engagement and offline service neglect, a link previously only studied via surveys.

proposals.title: Designing Judgment-Minimizing Interfaces: An Agent-Based Test Platform for Encouraging Positive Self-Disclosure
proposals.motivation: Social media and communication platforms often inadvertently foster social comparison and perceived judgment, which can stifle authentic sharing of positive experiences like humor and achievements. This is a critical design problem, as it reduces user engagement and well-being, particularly for users of assistive communication technologies where sharing is already effortful. Grounded in pre-2026 work on AAC instrumentation and generative AI's social potential, this research addresses how interface design can reshape communicative dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: Interface designs that de-emphasize quantifiable social metrics (e.g., likes, follower counts) and instead employ generative, conversational agents to provide contextual, affirming feedback will reduce users' perceived judgment and increase their comfort in sharing positive self-disclosures.
proposals.method: We will build a modular test platform where participants share text and image-based achievements/humor. Using a between-subjects design, we will vary interface conditions: a 'metric-heavy' control vs. experimental designs featuring (1) abstracted feedback (e.g., ambient color shifts) and (2) an interactive agent providing generated, affirming commentary. We will instrument the platform with Morris's established gaze-typing and behavior logging tools to measure hesitation and editing. Perceived judgment will be assessed via post-task surveys and linguistic analysis of shared content.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, instrumented platform for experimentally testing social-comparison theories in HCI. It will produce concrete design guidelines for reducing judgment in social-generative systems and demonstrate a methodology for integrating agent-based interaction with fine-grained behavioral measurement.
proposals.novelty: While social comparison is well-studied, this approach uniquely combines generative AI as a direct interface component to counteract judgment, and leverages detailed behavioral instrumentation (gaze, typing dynamics) from AAC research to measure subtle disclosure barriers not captured by surveys alone.

proposals.title: Mapping Support Flow: A Social Network Analysis of Information and Emotional Support Diffusion in Online AAC Communities
proposals.motivation: Online communities are vital for individuals using Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC), providing peer support and practical knowledge. However, the structure and dynamics of how both informational and emotional support diffuse through these networks are poorly understood. Characterizing this support flow is crucial for designing better digital platforms and generative AI tools that can facilitate, not disrupt, these critical social ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: Online AAC support networks exhibit distinct, measurable structural patterns for information diffusion versus emotional support exchange, and these patterns correlate with community resilience and member satisfaction.
proposals.method: We will instrument online forums and groups (e.g., Reddit, Facebook) using APIs to collect longitudinal interaction data (posts, replies, mentions, reactions). We will construct multiplex networks where layers represent different support types, identified via a BERT-based classifier fine-tuned on AAC communication corpora. Social network analysis metrics (e.g., centrality, community detection via the Louvain algorithm, tie strength, and diffusion models) will be applied to each layer and comparatively analyzed to map support flow pathways and bottlenecks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative model of support diffusion in AAC communities, extending prior descriptive work. It will yield design principles for social computing and generative AI agents (e.g., chatbots, facilitators) that are sensitive to existing support network structures, ensuring they augment rather than replace human-provided emotional support.
proposals.novelty: While SNA has been used in online health communities, this study uniquely focuses on the AAC context and employs a multiplex network approach to disentangle and directly compare the diffusion mechanics of informational versus emotional support, which are often conflated.

proposals.title: Generative Agent Simulation of Social Comparison and Stress in Online Support Groups
proposals.motivation: Online support groups are widely studied for their benefits, but pre-2026 research increasingly notes paradoxical harms, such as upward social comparison and anxiety from 'success stories'. Understanding the specific conversational dynamics and content that trigger these negative outcomes is crucial for designing healthier online spaces. This aligns with Morris's focus on social communication and generative AI's potential in modeling human behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in text-based online support groups, generative agents simulating members with varied recovery narratives will produce interaction patterns where exposure to highly positive, low-effort success posts increases simulated stress and self-judgment metrics in other agents, compared to exposure to balanced or struggle-focused narratives.
proposals.method: We will build a multi-agent simulation using a framework like Generative Agents, where each agent has a persona (e.g., recovery stage, coping style) and interacts in a simulated forum. Agents will be prompted with a large language model (e.g., GPT-4) and equipped with a 'well-being' state variable influenced by perceived social comparison. We will instrument the simulation to log gaze-like attention (which posts agents 'read') and linguistic behavior (their generated replies). A factorial design will vary the valence and effort framing of seed 'success' posts to measure changes in agent stress language and disengagement.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, generative AI-based methodology for modeling the negative psychosocial impacts of online communication at scale. It will produce a typology of harmful content triggers in support contexts and offer design implications for moderation systems and community guidelines that mitigate comparison-driven stress.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses surveys or logs to study online harm, this proposal uniquely uses generative agents as interactive, behavior-grounded simulants to causally test content effects, extending Morris's gaze-typing and agent-based methods to a new domain of digital well-being.

proposals.title: The Accessibility Bill of Materials (A11yBOM): A Generative, Agent-Based Framework for Enforcing Compliance as a Versioned Dependency
proposals.motivation: Despite decades of guidelines (WCAG), digital accessibility remains a persistent, costly post-hoc remediation problem, as evidenced by widespread non-compliance on platforms like Wikipedia. This is partly because accessibility is treated as a qualitative audit, not a quantifiable, enforceable technical dependency integrated into developer workflows, creating misaligned economic incentives.
proposals.hypothesis: Treating accessibility requirements as explicit, versioned dependencies in a machine-readable A11yBOM, and enforcing them via automated agents in CI/CD pipelines, will significantly reduce accessibility regressions and reframe compliance as a core engineering concern rather than a legal afterthought.
proposals.method: We will develop a schema for the A11yBOM, extending Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) concepts to encode WCAG success criteria as versioned constraints. A pipeline of testing agents, using pre-2026 tools like axe-core and Pa11y, will be orchestrated to evaluate web artifacts against the BOM. We will instrument this system in a longitudinal study with developers, using Morris's gaze/behavior methods from 'SpeakFaster Observer' to measure interaction with A11yBOM-generated feedback, and employ generative AI techniques (inspired by Morris's recent survey) to create natural-language explanations of failures and remediation costs.
proposals.contribution: 1) A formal, machine-actionable specification for accessibility dependencies (A11yBOM). 2) Empirical evidence on how quantifiable accessibility debt and automated enforcement alter developer behavior and project economics. 3) A novel integration of generative AI for explanatory feedback within a compliance toolchain.
proposals.novelty: It moves beyond static auditing by framing accessibility as a versioned, dependency-management problem, and uniquely combines SBOM architecture, agent-based automation, and generative communication to directly intervene in developer toolchains and incentives.

proposals.title: CAP-DEM: A Modular Framework for Accessibility Standards Inspired by Bioinformatics Data Models
proposals.motivation: Current digital accessibility standards, like WCAG, often face adoption friction due to their one-size-fits-all nature, which can be overly broad for specific domains or insufficiently detailed for others. This tension mirrors historical challenges in bioinformatics, where rigid universal data standards hindered specialized subfields. A more flexible, hierarchical model could improve both adoption and effectiveness across diverse industries like web development, scientific communication, and assistive technology.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical accessibility framework, consisting of a minimal, mandatory Core Accessibility Profile (CAP) and optional, community-developed Domain Extension Modules (DEMs), will be perceived as more adoptable and effective by practitioners than a monolithic standard, without leading to harmful fragmentation.
proposals.method: We will first conduct a structured analysis of bioinformatics standards (e.g., MIAME, FASTQ) to formalize their modular design principles. Using these principles, we will co-design a prototype CAP-DEM framework through participatory workshops with accessibility experts from three distinct domains: web content (aligned with WCAG), scientific figures (building on Morris's work on image accessibility), and real-time communication (informed by gaze-typing research). The framework's utility and perceived adoption barriers will be evaluated via a controlled survey and agent-based simulation modeling the diffusion of standards across a synthetic community of developers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, principled model for structuring flexible accessibility standards, grounded in a successful analogy from another field. It will deliver a concrete prototype framework and empirical data on its potential to reduce adoption friction while maintaining interoperability, contributing to both HCI and standards governance literature.
proposals.novelty: While prior work critiques monolithic standards, this research uniquely proposes and tests a specific, bioinformatics-inspired architectural solution (CAP-DEM). It moves beyond critique to a generative, testable design framework, integrating Morris's expertise in cross-linguistic accessibility and generative AI's potential for domain-specific guideline creation.

proposals.title: Cross-Domain Accessibility Pattern Transfer: A Generative Agent Framework for Post-Development Integration
proposals.motivation: Despite advances in accessibility guidelines (e.g., WCAG), retrofitting legacy software and complex systems remains prohibitively expensive and often ineffective due to a lack of contextual solutions. This creates a critical gap, as shown by Morris's work on image accessibility disparities across languages, where universal standards fail to address specific use-case needs. A pragmatic method for integrating proven, domain-specific patterns post-hoc is urgently needed to improve real-world access without foundational re-architecting.
proposals.hypothesis: That a curated library of high-efficacy, domain-specific accessibility patterns, coupled with a systematic method for cross-domain adaptation, can enable effective post-development accessibility integration where generic standards fail.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where generative AI agents, informed by pre-2026 models like GPT-4, analyze documented patterns (e.g., tactile feedback in automotive HMI, gaze-controlled navigation from AAC research) and propose adaptations for a target domain (e.g., enterprise software). The method involves: 1) constructing a pattern library from literature and case studies, 2) training agents to map functional intent and user behavior (e.g., via simulated gaze or interaction data) across domains, and 3) validating proposed adaptations through expert evaluation (inspired by Morris's scientist survey method) and rapid prototyping with tools like Slide to assess integration feasibility.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, actionable framework for pragmatic accessibility retrofitting, moving beyond compliance to contextual efficacy. It will yield a publicly available, cross-domain pattern library and demonstrate how generative AI can be strategically used for design synthesis in accessibility, bridging social (communication needs) and technical (generative, agent-based) research themes.
proposals.novelty: It shifts the focus from creating new foundational standards to the intelligent transfer and adaptation of existing, proven solutions across domains, a non-obvious approach given the prevailing emphasis on 'shift-left' and universal design. The use of generative agents for cross-domain pattern mapping in accessibility is an unexplored synthesis of Morris's prior work on agents, behavior, and generative AI potential.

proposals.title: Generative Agents for Simulating Domain-Specific vs. Cross-Industry Accessibility Guideline Adoption
proposals.motivation: Current digital accessibility efforts are guided by broad standards like WCAG, but their uniform application can be inefficient for specialized domains like scientific communication, AAC devices, or multilingual platforms. Pre-2026, the rise of generative AI and agent-based modeling offers a new way to simulate the real-world adoption and impact of guidelines, building on Morris's prior work in gaze-based AAC, image accessibility, and scientists' views on AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a set of fragmented, domain-specific accessibility guidelines, generated and refined by AI agents simulating stakeholder behavior, will lead to measurably better simulated user outcomes (e.g., communication speed, task completion) than the application of a monolithic, cross-industry standard.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent simulation framework where agents, endowed with behavioral models from prior gaze-typing studies, enact roles (e.g., scientist, AAC user, Wikipedia editor). Using a large language model (e.g., GPT-4) as a generative engine, agents will interact with digital content under two conditions: one governed by WCAG, and another by dynamically evolved, domain-specific rules. The system will use reinforcement learning to iteratively refine the domain-specific guidelines based on simulated interaction logs and success metrics, directly testing their efficacy.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, generative agent-based methodology for evaluating and designing accessibility standards. It will produce concrete evidence on the trade-offs between unified and fragmented guidelines, offering a new, simulation-driven tool for policy and design decisions in human-computer interaction and accessibility research.
proposals.novelty: While guidelines are often studied via user testing, using generative AI agents to simulate long-term, cross-domain adoption and emergent guideline optimization is a novel approach. It merges Morris's expertise in instrumentation, generative AI perspectives, and accessibility into a new experimental paradigm.

proposals.title: Modeling the Developer's Dilemma: An Agent-Based Simulation of Inclusive Design Costs and Market Benefits
proposals.motivation: While inclusive design is an ethical imperative, its adoption by developers is often hindered by perceived high upfront costs and uncertain returns. Pre-2026, the business case for accessibility is largely anecdotal or based on static market analyses, failing to capture the dynamic, generative nature of how inclusive features can reshape user adoption and communication in digital ecosystems. This gap makes it difficult to provide concrete, data-driven guidance to developers on when and how inclusive design yields a positive return on investment.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the cost-benefit of inclusive design is not universally positive but follows a predictable, context-dependent curve, where initial investments yield disproportionately high market expansion for products in social and communicative domains, but with diminishing returns after core accessibility barriers are removed.
proposals.method: We will build a multi-agent simulation where AI agents, endowed with heterogeneous abilities (modeled on data from tools like SpeakFaster Observer for gaze-typing behavior), interact with and adopt a software product. We will parameterize developer agents who can invest in inclusive features (e.g., alternative input, generative AI for content simplification) at varying costs. The core algorithm will simulate market diffusion, where agent adoption is a function of product utility, which is modulated by the match between the agent's ability profile and the product's inclusive features. We will systematically vary cost parameters, market composition, and product type (social app vs. utility tool) to map the cost-benefit landscape.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, generative model for reasoning about inclusive design economics, moving beyond case studies. It will provide a methodological framework for simulating design decisions and offer empirical, scenario-based evidence on the market conditions under which inclusivity is most beneficial. The findings will be packaged into actionable insights for developers and designers.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying agent-based modeling and behavioral simulation—methods used in HCI for studying interaction—specifically to the economic calculus of inclusive design, integrating real-world ability data (e.g., gaze-typing patterns) into a dynamic market model.

proposals.title: From Compliance to Catalyst: Quantifying Market Expansion and Innovation Spillovers of Ability-Based VR Design
proposals.motivation: The VR industry, pre-2026, faces a tension between rapid innovation and inclusive design, often treating accessibility as a costly retrofit. This limits market reach and may stifle broader UX innovation. Grounded in principles of ability-based design and generative research, this study addresses a critical gap: a lack of empirical, longitudinal data on the commercial and technical returns of building VR systems from the outset for diverse abilities.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that VR projects employing foundational ability-based design paradigms will demonstrate superior market performance (broader adoption, higher retention) and will generate measurable secondary innovation spillovers—novel interaction techniques and improved universal UX—compared to projects using a retrofit approach.
proposals.method: We will instrument a longitudinal panel of 8-10 nascent VR projects (e.g., using Unity/Unreal) via a mixed-methods approach. Quantitative telemetry (user logs, A/B testing) will track market metrics. To measure innovation spillovers, we will deploy Morris's 'SpeakFaster Observer'-inspired instrumentation to capture fine-grained interaction data (gaze, controller behavior, agent-assisted inputs). A novel analysis pipeline will use sequence mining and agent-based simulation to identify and trace the provenance of novel interaction patterns from edge-case to mainstream adoption.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first longitudinal dataset linking inclusive design to market and innovation outcomes in VR. It will yield a validated cost-benefit model for stakeholders and a methodological framework for quantifying secondary innovation, shifting the discourse on inclusive design from ethical obligation to core technical strategy.
proposals.novelty: While prior work characterizes accessibility gaps or advocates for inclusive design, this study is novel in its longitudinal, instrumented measurement of *innovation spillovers* as a direct return on investment, applying behavioral telemetry methods from AAC research to the VR development pipeline.

proposals.title: Augmenting Social Communication: A Generative Agent Framework for Ability Augmentation in VR
proposals.motivation: Prior work in Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) and accessibility, such as eye-gaze typing instrumentation, focuses on restoring function to a normative baseline. However, the potential of VR to augment human abilities beyond typical ranges—enhancing perception, social cognition, or expressive bandwidth—remains underexplored. This research addresses the dual problem of design: moving beyond compensation to create experiences that expand human capability, a frontier highlighted by emerging interest in generative AI's potential across scientific fields.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a VR framework integrating generative AI agents and real-time user behavior modeling (e.g., gaze, gesture) can be designed to measurably augment a user's social communication ability, such as increasing the speed, nuance, or multi-modal coherence of their interactions.
proposals.method: We will develop a VR testbed where users engage in structured social scenarios (e.g., negotiation, storytelling) with AI-driven agent partners. The system will instrument user behavior (gaze, speech, gesture) using pre-2026 methods like the Observer framework. A central generative language model (e.g., a fine-tuned GPT-3/4 or BART architecture) will power adaptive agent responses and propose real-time communication augmentations—such as predictive sentence completion, suggested emotional tone overlays, or visual highlighting of social cues—based on the user's modeled behavior and interaction history.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel VR framework and metrics for evaluating ability augmentation (not just restoration), 2) empirical data on how generative AI can be harnessed to expand human communication, and 3) design principles for 'augmentation-first' interfaces, extending the researcher's prior work on instrumentation and cross-language accessibility.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in shifting the design paradigm from ability compensation to augmentation within social communication, and in the technical integration of generative AI agents with continuous behavioral instrumentation to create a closed-loop, adaptive augmentation system.

proposals.title: Cross-Lingual Safety and Groundedness: Extending RLHF and Knowledge-Augmented Generation to Low-Resource Languages
proposals.motivation: As generative language models proliferate globally, their safety and factual reliability remain concentrated on English, creating significant risks for non-English speakers. Pre-2026 research on Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) has demonstrated efficacy in English but requires adaptation for linguistic and cultural contexts where high-quality safety annotations and knowledge sources are scarce. This work addresses a critical equity gap in AI safety and accessibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multi-stage fine-tuning pipeline combining cross-lingual transfer learning with targeted, culturally-contextual human feedback can produce safer and more factually grounded generative models for low-resource languages, without requiring massive parallel datasets.
proposals.method: We will adapt established methods like RLHF and RAG for multiple target languages (e.g., Swahili, Bengali, Tamil). Using a base multilingual model (e.g., mT5 or BLOOM), we will first generate synthetic prompts and responses for safety evaluation. We will then implement a culturally-situated annotation protocol, potentially using the researcher's expertise in gaze and behavioral methods to efficiently collect human feedback on harmfulness and hallucination. This feedback will train safety and grounding classifiers, which will be integrated into a RAG system that queries language-specific knowledge sources (e.g., localized Wikipedia snapshots).
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a scalable framework for cross-lingual safety fine-tuning, 2) a novel dataset of human feedback on model outputs for several low-resource languages, and 3) empirical results on the efficacy of knowledge-augmentation for reducing hallucinations across linguistic contexts. It directly extends the researcher's prior work on image accessibility across languages to the domain of generative text safety.
proposals.novelty: While multilingual models exist, systematically porting the full stack of safety and grounding techniques—particularly RLHF's human-in-the-loop refinement—to low-resource languages is an underexplored challenge. The novelty lies in the integrated, culturally-contextual approach to feedback collection and the application of these methods beyond high-resource linguistic settings.

proposals.title: Generating and Validating Nuanced Human Value Sets for AI Safety via Multi-Agent, Gaze-Informed Simulation
proposals.motivation: Current AI safety metrics often rely on coarse or Western-centric value sets, limiting their ability to detect subtle harms or represent diverse cultural perspectives. This is critical as generative models become deeply embedded in social and communicative tasks, from assistive technology to collaborative design. A more comprehensive, empirically-grounded taxonomy of human values is needed to quantify safety in a way that reflects real-world complexity and variance.
proposals.hypothesis: Simulating human communication and evaluation behaviors within a multi-agent framework, informed by real behavioral signals like gaze, can generate a more nuanced, context-sensitive, and actionable set of human values for safety evaluation than static, expert-defined lists.
proposals.method: We will design a multi-agent simulation where LLM-based agents, parameterized with different cultural and social perspectives (drawn from pre-2026 cross-linguistic datasets like Wikipedia image accessibility), engage in structured dialogue and collaborative design tasks. To ground value elicitation in human behavior, we will instrument the simulation with a virtual 'gaze' mechanism, inspired by the SpeakFaster Observer methodology, to track agents' 'attention' to different ethical dimensions during task execution and conflict resolution. The resulting interaction logs and attention maps will be analyzed using clustering and concept induction algorithms (e.g., non-negative matrix factorization) to extract a hierarchical value set.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, procedurally generated dataset of contextually-situated human values and a corresponding methodological framework for dynamic value elicitation. It contributes to the field of AI safety by providing a more granular and empirically-derived metric for model alignment, and to HCI by demonstrating how simulated behavioral signals can inform the design of evaluative systems.
proposals.novelty: The approach uniquely combines multi-agent social simulation with a computational model of behavioral attention (gaze) to generate safety values, moving beyond survey-based or top-down philosophical approaches. It directly integrates Morris's expertise in instrumentation, cross-linguistic analysis, and generative AI perspectives into the core safety evaluation pipeline.

proposals.title: Modeling the Adoption Curve: A Longitudinal, Agent-Based Study of Alt-Text Writing Behavior After Interface Feature Enablement
proposals.motivation: The persistent inaccessibility of images online, documented in work like 'Characterizing Image Accessibility on Wikipedia across Languages,' is a critical social and communication barrier. While platforms deploy features to prompt or simplify alt-text writing (e.g., AI suggestions, required fields), their long-term efficacy in changing user behavior is unknown. Understanding the dynamics of adoption—whether users internalize the practice or disengage—is essential for designing effective, generative communication ecosystems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the introduction of a new alt-text authoring feature will produce distinct, longitudinal behavioral trajectories among users, which can be modeled and predicted by a combination of individual interaction patterns (e.g., gaze, editing pauses) and social network exposure.
proposals.method: We will instrument a platform's image upload interface to log detailed interaction data (clicks, keystrokes, gaze via webcam using methods akin to SpeakFaster Observer, and exposure to AI-generated alt-text suggestions). Over 6-12 months, we will track a cohort of users. Using hierarchical Bayesian models, we will cluster individual longitudinal adoption curves. We will then train an agent-based model (ABM), where simulated agents' behavior rules (e.g., propensity to write, reliance on AI) are informed by the empirical clusters, to test how different feature designs might amplify or sustain adoption.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel longitudinal dataset of alt-text authoring behavior and a computational framework for modeling the adoption of pro-social design features. It will provide concrete design guidelines for sustaining accessible communication practices, moving beyond simple compliance metrics to understand habit formation.
proposals.novelty: This study uniquely combines longitudinal behavioral logging (common in AAC research) with agent-based modeling to understand design feature adoption, a method not previously applied to the alt-text domain. It focuses on the dynamics of behavior change over time, rather than just snapshot compliance.

proposals.title: Characterizing the Practices of Image Description Advocates: A Mixed-Methods Study of Power Users on Wikipedia
proposals.motivation: Despite the critical importance of alt-text for web accessibility, most images online lack descriptions, creating significant barriers. Prior large-scale analyses, such as Morris's work on cross-lingual Wikipedia image accessibility, have quantified this gap but offer limited insight into how to effectively cultivate and sustain the volunteer labor needed to close it. Understanding the motivations, strategies, and behaviors of the small cohort of highly productive contributors is essential for designing systems that can recruit and support more describers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that power users of image description tools employ a distinct, teachable set of cognitive and social strategies that differ from those of casual contributors, and that these strategies are key to their high output and quality.
proposals.method: We will conduct a longitudinal, mixed-methods study. First, we will algorithmically identify the top 1% of image describers across multiple Wikipedia language editions using edit history logs. For these power users and a matched control group, we will instrument their description-writing sessions using a browser extension modeled on the SpeakFaster Observer to capture fine-grained behavioral data (e.g., gaze patterns on the image vs. text field, editing latency). We will then deploy conversational AI agents, built on pre-2026 large language models (e.g., GPT-4), to conduct structured, in-depth interviews with these users, probing their decision-making processes and social advocacy roles.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, data-driven taxonomy of effective image description practices and a behavioral model of high-efficiency contribution. It will contribute to the fields of human-computer interaction and accessibility by providing evidence-based design guidelines for the next generation of generative AI-assisted description tools, aimed at amplifying user effort rather than replacing it.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has measured contribution volume or used surveys, this study uniquely combines long-term behavioral instrumentation (gaze/typing) with AI-facilitated qualitative interviews to uncover the tacit expertise of advocates, moving beyond scale to study depth of practice.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Generative Sign Language Agents: A Participatory Framework for Accessible Communication Technology
proposals.motivation: Sign language technologies, such as AI-powered avatars, often fail to meet the nuanced communication needs of Deaf and Hard-of-Hearing (DHH) communities due to a lack of deep user involvement in the design process. This disconnect leads to tools that are misaligned with cultural and linguistic preferences, hindering true accessibility. Participatory design (PD) from HCI offers a proven pathway to center DHH users, but its application to emerging generative AI for sign language remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: That a structured participatory design process, co-creating with DHH signers, will yield a set of design principles and prototype agent behaviors for generative sign language technology that are significantly more usable, culturally appropriate, and trusted than those developed through expert-driven methods alone.
proposals.method: We will conduct a multi-stage participatory design study with 15-20 DHH participants. Sessions will employ adapted PD methods: (1) **Future Workshops** to critique current tech and envision ideal agents; (2) **Layered Elaboration** with storyboards and video prototypes to specify agent behavior (e.g., pacing, non-manual signals); (3) **Co-creation with a configurable simulation** using a high-fidelity signing avatar (e.g., building on platforms like SignLab or using game engines) to iteratively adjust generative parameters (sequence length, expression intensity) based on user feedback. Data (video, gaze patterns from screen recording, design artifacts) will be analyzed thematically.
