proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a detailed PD methodology tailored for generative sign technology and a concrete set of co-designed interaction guidelines for signing agents. It will advance the field of accessible computing by providing a robust, community-grounded framework for developing user-centered AAC and communication tools, moving beyond technical performance metrics to embrace social validity.
proposals.novelty: While PD is known in HCI, its application to the specific challenges of *generative* AI for sign language—where output is dynamic and non-deterministic—is novel. It uniquely combines Morris's expertise in long-term instrumentation (e.g., gaze typing) and generative AI perspectives with deep co-design to address the social and communicative nuances of sign language.

proposals.title: Beyond Information Retrieval: Modeling and Measuring Social-Bonding Queries in Conversational AI
proposals.motivation: Dominant models of human-AI interaction, especially in search and conversational agents, assume information-seeking is the primary user goal. This overlooks a fundamental aspect of human communication: using queries to build social rapport, express shared identity, or manage conversation flow, not just to obtain facts. Understanding this 'social querying' is crucial for designing more natural, engaging, and supportive AI communication partners, particularly in assistive and collaborative contexts.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a significant portion of queries in open-ended human-AI dialogue are primarily social-bonding oriented, and that these can be reliably distinguished from information-seeking queries through a multimodal model integrating linguistic, paralinguistic, and user behavioral signals.
proposals.method: We will develop a multi-agent simulation framework where LLM-based user agents, imbued with varied social personas, interact with a target conversational AI. We will instrument these interactions to log query text, synthetic 'gaze' patterns (simulating attention), and turn-taking behavior. A classifier will be trained to label query intent (social-bonding vs. information-seeking), using features from the query, dialogue history, and simulated behavioral cues. We will validate this model through a controlled lab study with human participants, using eye-tracking (extending the SpeakFaster Observer methodology) and interaction logs to ground-truth social querying behavior.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a novel taxonomy and labeled dataset of social-bonding queries in human-AI conversation, 2) a validated multimodal model for real-time intent classification, and 3) design implications for generative AI systems to recognize and appropriately respond to social goals, thereby improving communication quality and user satisfaction.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the core information-retrieval assumption in conversational AI research by formally modeling social querying as a primary objective. It uniquely combines Morris's expertise in behavioral instrumentation (gaze) and generative agents to create a novel experimental paradigm for studying social communication goals.

proposals.title: Need-Based Quality Assessment: A Comparative Analysis of Search Engine and Social Q&A Platform Performance
proposals.motivation: People seek information from diverse sources, primarily search engines and social Q&A platforms, but the comparative quality of answers across different types of information needs is poorly understood. This gap limits our ability to design effective information retrieval systems and communication tools, especially for assistive and generative technologies that must prioritize reliable information sourcing. A systematic, need-based analysis is crucial for guiding users and system designers.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the perceived quality and objective accuracy of answers will differ significantly between search engines and social Q&A platforms, and that this difference will be systematically moderated by the type of information need (e.g., factual, procedural, opinion-based, or complex explanatory).
proposals.method: We will first establish a taxonomy of information needs by extending existing frameworks and validating it via a survey with domain experts. We will then instrument a study using a custom web platform to present participants with categorized information needs. For each need, participants will be shown paired results: one from a simulated search engine result page (using a standard API like Google Custom Search) and one from a social Q&A platform (e.g., a Reddit or Stack Exchange thread). We will measure answer quality through both objective metrics (fact-checking, completeness) and subjective user ratings, employing statistical models (e.g., mixed-effects regression) to analyze the interaction between source, need type, and quality.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, fine-grained taxonomy of information needs for evaluating answer quality. It will provide empirical evidence on the strengths and weaknesses of algorithmic search versus socially-generated content for different needs, offering concrete design implications for the next generation of assistive communication tools, conversational agents, and information retrieval systems.
proposals.novelty: While prior work compares platforms generically, this study's novelty lies in its systematic, need-based lens and its integration of objective and subjective quality measures, directly linking information science to human-computer interaction design for communication aids.

proposals.title: Channel Choice in Information Seeking: A Multi-Modal Study of Situational and Psychological Triggers for Search vs. Social Queries
proposals.motivation: The rise of generative AI and large language models (pre-2026) is reshaping information ecosystems, yet fundamental questions remain about how people choose between algorithmic search and social querying (e.g., asking a person or community). Understanding the psychological and situational drivers behind this choice is critical for designing equitable, supportive communication and information systems, especially for assistive technology and collaborative work.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the choice to use a search engine versus a social query is primarily driven by a combination of perceived query complexity, the searcher's current affective state (e.g., urgency, anxiety), and their assessment of the required answer's subjectivity, rather than purely by topic or availability.
proposals.method: We will instrument participants' devices with a modified 'Observer' framework, inspired by Morris's gaze-typing work, to log search/social app usage and context. A lab study will use a gaze-contingent interface to present controlled information-seeking tasks while measuring eye movements and galvanic skin response as proxies for cognitive load and affective state. We will employ a multi-agent simulation architecture, where LLM-based agents (e.g., using GPT-4 or similar pre-2026 models) enact varied situational parameters to generate a large corpus of synthetic but grounded decision logs, which will be used to train a behavioral prediction model to identify key decision triggers.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a validated, multi-factor model of information channel choice, moving beyond simple descriptive surveys. It will also provide a novel methodological blueprint combining long-term behavioral instrumentation, psychophysiological sensing, and agent-based simulation for studying communication decisions.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of continuous psychophysiological measurement (gaze, arousal) with agent-based behavioral simulation to model a high-level communication choice, a method not previously applied to this specific problem in human-computer interaction.

proposals.title: Search vs. Social: A Longitudinal Diary Study of In-Situ Information-Seeking Motivations and Outcomes
proposals.motivation: People increasingly turn to social platforms (e.g., X, Reddit) for information seeking, not just search engines, but the longitudinal, contextual factors driving this choice are poorly understood. Prior work in HCI and information retrieval has largely relied on lab studies or log analysis, missing the nuanced, situated motivations and evolving outcomes of these decisions in daily life. Understanding this dynamic is crucial for designing future communication and information systems that better support users' needs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the choice between search and social querying is driven by a combination of contextual factors (e.g., task complexity, time pressure, perceived expertise needed) and that longitudinal engagement with one modality shapes trust and future use patterns in non-linear ways.
proposals.method: We will conduct a 4-week technology-mediated diary study with 30-40 participants, recruiting from platforms like Prolific. Participants will report multiple information-seeking episodes per week via a custom mobile app, capturing their query, chosen platform (search/social), motivation, immediate outcome, and affective state. Inspired by Morris's prior work on longitudinal instrumentation (SpeakFaster Observer) and agent-based modeling, we will augment self-reports with passive, privacy-preserving device logging (app usage timestamps) to ground responses. Data analysis will employ mixed methods: thematic coding of motivations and outcomes, and statistical modeling (e.g., multilevel logistic regression) to identify predictors of platform choice over time.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rich, longitudinal dataset and framework for understanding hybrid search-social information seeking. It will contribute empirical findings on the contextual drivers and personal outcomes of this behavior, and offer methodological insights for longitudinal HCI research on communication and information access.
proposals.novelty: While diary studies are known in HCI, their application to directly compare search and social querying longitudinally, combined with lightweight behavioral logging to contextualize self-reports, is novel. This bridges Morris's expertise in longitudinal gaze-typing instrumentation and generative AI perceptions with core questions in social information access.

proposals.title: Cognitive Load in Information Foraging: A Comparative Study of Search Engine Use and Social Q&A Evaluation
proposals.motivation: People increasingly rely on both search engines and social Q&A platforms (e.g., Stack Overflow, Reddit) for information, but the cognitive demands of these distinct channels are poorly understood. As generative AI begins to produce search-like and social-like answers, understanding the baseline mental effort required to process information from these channels is crucial for designing future communication and information systems that minimize user burden.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that evaluating answers from social Q&A platforms imposes a higher intrinsic cognitive load than performing a directed web search, due to the need to assess source credibility, argument quality, and potential bias in less structured content.
proposals.method: We will conduct a controlled lab study where participants complete information-seeking tasks via a custom instrumented platform. Cognitive load will be measured multi-modally: 1) using a dual-task paradigm with a secondary auditory reaction time task, 2) pupillometry via a Tobii eye-tracker to capture gaze-based indices of cognitive effort, and 3) subjective NASA-TLX ratings. The platform will log interaction behavior (queries, clicks, dwell time). We will compare load measures across the two conditions (search vs. social answer evaluation) using mixed-effects models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a foundational, empirical comparison of the cognitive costs of two major online information channels. It will contribute a novel, multi-modal dataset of cognitive load during information foraging and offer design implications for blending search and social interfaces, as well as for calibrating generative AI outputs to match the cognitive affordances of different channels.
proposals.novelty: While cognitive load theory has been applied to search interfaces, a direct, instrumented comparison with social information evaluation is novel. The approach uniquely combines Morris's expertise in long-term gaze instrumentation (from AAC work) and analysis of social/generative content to a new problem in human-information interaction.

proposals.title: Generative Queries and Communicative Gaps: A Large-Scale Log Analysis of Information-Seeking Behavior Across Social and Search Platforms
proposals.motivation: Prior work, such as characterizing image accessibility on Wikipedia, reveals systemic gaps in how information is represented across languages and cultures. The rise of generative AI for search and communication, as noted in scientists' perspectives, is fundamentally changing how people formulate queries and seek information. A large-scale, comparative analysis of actual search and social media logs is needed to empirically map these evolving behaviors and identify mismatches between public discourse and information needs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that query topics and linguistic structures in search logs will systematically differ from those in social media posts, revealing distinct 'information-seeking' versus 'discourse-shaping' modes. Furthermore, we hypothesize that the volume and nature of queries related to generative AI tools will correlate with user demographics and platform-specific communicative norms.
proposals.method: We will analyze two massive, anonymized log datasets: (1) a commercial web search query log, and (2) a corpus of public social media posts (e.g., from Twitter/X or Reddit). We will employ transformer-based language models (e.g., BERT, RoBERTa) for topic modeling and fine-grained classification of queries/posts. Agent-based simulation techniques, inspired by Morris's prior work, will be used to model the flow of topics between platforms. Gaze-typing instrumentation concepts from 'SpeakFaster Observer' inform our analysis of query composition efficiency and reformulation patterns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first large-scale, cross-platform characterization of how generative AI is reshaping query formulation. It will contribute a novel methodological framework for log analysis that integrates NLP, agent-based simulation, and concepts from AAC research. The findings will offer concrete design implications for next-generation search engines, conversational agents, and assistive communication tools.
proposals.novelty: While log analysis is common, the direct comparison of search and social media logs through the lens of *generative communication* and *accessibility* is novel. Integrating methods from gaze-based AAC research into the analysis of massive behavioral logs creates a unique bridge between micro-level communication studies and macro-scale computational social science.

proposals.title: Generative Social Agents for Accessible VR: A Multi-Modal Input Framework Combining Gaze, Voice, and Head Gestures
proposals.motivation: Virtual Reality (VR) interaction remains largely dependent on handheld controllers, which can be inaccessible for users with motor impairments and disrupt social presence by occupying the hands. Pre-2026, research on alternative inputs like gaze and voice in VR has been siloed, focusing on single modalities. A unified, generative framework is needed to create more natural, accessible, and socially conducive interaction paradigms that move beyond the controller.
proposals.hypothesis: A generative agent-based system that fuses gaze, voice, and deliberate head gestures into a coherent interaction model will enable more accessible, efficient, and socially appropriate VR interactions than controller-based or single-modality input methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a VR testbed instrumented with eye-tracking (like the Gaze Typing methodology from 'SpeakFaster Observer'), microphone, and headset orientation tracking. A rule-based and neural (e.g., LSTM) multi-modal fusion architecture will interpret inputs: gaze for selection, voice for command, and head gestures (e.g., nod, shake) for confirmation/denial. We will train generative language models (e.g., GPT-3.5/4 API available pre-2026) as social agents to interpret and respond to natural voice commands within the VR context. A within-subjects study will evaluate this framework against a baseline controller condition on metrics of task efficiency, error rate, cognitive load, and perceived social presence.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel, open-source multi-modal fusion architecture for accessible VR input, 2) empirical data on the performance and user experience of controller-free interaction, and 3) a design framework for integrating generative AI as a social intermediary in VR, extending the lab's work on AI in scientific communication to human-computer interaction.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integration of the researcher's expertise in long-term gaze instrumentation and generative AI with VR interaction, specifically using a generative social agent to interpret and mediate a continuous, multi-modal input stream, rather than treating modalities in isolation.

proposals.title: Co-Designing Embodied Interaction: A Participatory Framework for Accessible VR Hardware Prototyping with Users with Limited Mobility
proposals.motivation: Virtual Reality (VR) holds immense potential for social connection, education, and therapy, yet mainstream hardware often excludes people with limited mobility due to reliance on precise, two-handed gestures and full-body movement. Pre-2026, accessible VR research is nascent, frequently adopting a top-down 'solution-for' rather than 'solution-with' approach, risking misalignment with real user needs and contexts. This work addresses a critical gap in inclusive design for immersive technologies, building on principles of Universal Design and Morris's prior work on instrumenting assistive communication.
proposals.hypothesis: That a structured participatory design process, co-led by users with limited mobility, will yield VR hardware interaction prototypes that are significantly more accessible, usable, and contextually relevant than those developed through expert-led methods alone. We further hypothesize that this process will surface novel, embodied interaction paradigms not evident in conventional VR design.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of iterative co-design workshops with 8-10 participants with diverse limited mobility conditions (e.g., cerebral palsy, spinal cord injury). Sessions will utilize low-fidelity prototyping materials (e.g., clay, sensors, microcontrollers) and bodystorming in mixed-reality environments. Building on Morris's expertise in gaze and behavior instrumentation, we will instrument prototypes with sensors to quantitatively log interaction attempts and difficulties. We will employ thematic analysis of workshop transcripts and sensor logs, using an agent-based modeling framework (e.g., simulating interaction sequences) to generalize findings into design principles.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, documented participatory design framework specifically for accessible VR hardware, 2) a portfolio of low-fidelity, user-generated prototype concepts for accessible interaction, and 3) a set of empirically grounded design guidelines and challenge areas for the HCI and VR communities. It will advance the field of generative design by demonstrating how user expertise can directly 'generate' physical interaction solutions.
proposals.novelty: While participatory design is known in HCI, its application to the physical co-creation of VR hardware with disabled users is novel. The integration of quantitative behavioral instrumentation (gaze, movement logs) within a qualitative co-design process to ground design principles in observable interaction data represents a significant methodological innovation beyond typical workshop summaries.

proposals.title: Generative Agents for Adaptive VR: An Ability-Based Design Framework for Personalized Motor Interaction
proposals.motivation: Virtual Reality (VR) holds immense promise for social connection, education, and rehabilitation, but its reliance on standard controllers creates significant accessibility barriers for users with diverse motor abilities. Pre-2026, ability-based design in HCI has shown success in 2D interfaces, but its application to immersive 3D environments remains underexplored. This gap limits VR's inclusivity and fails to leverage its potential for truly personalized interaction.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a VR system using a generative AI agent to model individual motor capabilities and dynamically adapt interaction techniques in real-time will significantly improve performance and subjective experience for users across a wide spectrum of motor abilities, compared to a one-size-fits-all controller scheme.
proposals.method: We will develop a VR framework where a user's interaction (via gaze, gesture, or alternative input) is continuously analyzed by a monitoring agent, building on methods from the 'SpeakFaster Observer' for longitudinal behavior modeling. A central generative AI agent, inspired by large language models' capacity for task planning, will use this ability model to synthesize and propose context-aware interaction adaptations (e.g., adjusting selection dwell times, remapping gestures, or generating simplified object manipulation affordances). We will evaluate the system through a controlled study where participants with varied motor capabilities complete standardized communication and object-manipulation tasks in VR.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel, implemented framework for ability-based, adaptive interaction in VR, 2) empirical data on the efficacy of generative agents for real-time accessibility adaptation, and 3) design guidelines for creating VR experiences that are inherently flexible to user capability, advancing the field beyond static accessibility settings.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in integrating generative AI agents—typically used for content creation—as core mediators of accessibility, moving beyond pre-scripted adaptations to create a dynamic, learning system that personalizes VR interaction in real-time based on continuous ability assessment.

proposals.title: Beyond Assistance: A Generative Agent Framework for Modeling and Measuring Potential Harms in AI-Powered AAC Systems
proposals.motivation: Research and development of AI for people with disabilities (PWD), particularly in Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC), is overwhelmingly framed through an assistive, benefit-centric lens. This dominant narrative, accelerated by generative AI, risks overlooking subtle but significant harms related to agency, identity, and social dynamics. A critical, empirical gap exists in proactively characterizing these potential harms before systems are widely deployed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that generative AI models, when integrated into AAC systems, can introduce measurable harms beyond their assistive utility, including the erosion of communicative authenticity, increased social burden on interlocutors, and the imposition of normative linguistic styles that conflict with user identity.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation environment using generative AI agents (e.g., based on LLMs like GPT-4 or LLaMA) to model dyadic conversations between a simulated AAC user and a communication partner. The AAC agent's generation will be parameterized to reflect different AI-assistive strategies (e.g., sentence completion, style transfer). We will instrument these simulations with Morris's established gaze-typing logging methods (from SpeakFaster Observer) and novel interaction metrics to quantify conversational friction, turn-taking imbalance, and semantic drift from user intent. Harm will be operationalized via these metrics and validated through targeted workshops with AAC users and clinicians.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel generative agent-based methodology for pre-deployment harm assessment in assistive technology, 2) a preliminary taxonomy and metrics for 'AAC harm,' moving the field beyond pure performance evaluation, and 3) design implications for generative AAC that center user sovereignty alongside assistive efficiency.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in applying a critical, simulation-driven, harm-modeling lens to AI for disability—a domain typically insulated from such scrutiny—and in repurpose generative agents from creative tools to probes for identifying communicative risk.

proposals.title: Auditing with Care: A Participatory Multi-Axis Ontology for Contextual Disability Bias in Generative AI
proposals.motivation: Current AI bias auditing often treats disability as a monolithic, static attribute, failing to capture its contextual, intersectional, and interactional nature. This leads to audits that are conceptually misaligned with the lived experiences of disabled people and may miss critical failure modes in systems like generative image models, language models, or computer vision. Grounded in pre-2026 work on participatory AI and accessibility, this research addresses the urgent need for auditing frameworks developed with and for the disability community.
proposals.hypothesis: A disability ontology, co-constructed with disabled communities and structured across multiple axes (e.g., impairment type, assistive tech use, environmental context), will enable the generation of more valid, actionable, and context-specific bias tests for automated systems than audits based on simplistic or transferred protected attributes.
proposals.method: We will conduct participatory design workshops with disabled experts and AAC users, informed by Morris's prior work on gaze-typing instrumentation and image accessibility. Workshop outputs will structure a formal, multi-axis ontology. This ontology will then drive a rule-based and LLM-augmented (e.g., GPT-4) test-generation system to produce adversarial prompts and evaluation criteria for auditing generative models (e.g., DALL-E, Stable Diffusion) and classifiers. The system's behavior will be evaluated by comparing audit findings from our ontology-driven tests against those from standard attribute-based audits.
proposals.contribution: This work contributes 1) a novel, publicly available participatory ontology for disability in algorithmic systems, 2) an open-source auditing framework that uses this ontology to generate contextual bias tests, and 3) empirical findings on bias in generative AI models across specific disability-related contexts previously unexamined.
proposals.novelty: This moves beyond applying fairness notions from race or gender to disability by fundamentally re-grounding the audit's conceptual framework in participatory disability knowledge. It integrates Morris's expertise in instrumentation, communication, and generative AI to operationalize this context-sensitive auditing.

proposals.title: Indicator Subgroups for Algorithmic Audits: A Disability Bias Framework Inspired by Ecological Monitoring
proposals.motivation: Auditing AI systems for bias against people with disabilities is critically important but methodologically challenging due to the vast heterogeneity within the disability community. Pre-2026, bias auditing frameworks often treat disability as a monolithic category or rely on synthetic proxies, failing to capture real-world impacts on specific user groups. This gap hinders the development of equitable generative AI and communication technologies, which are central to accessibility.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that specific, well-defined disability subgroups (e.g., screen reader users, switch users) can serve as valid 'indicator subgroups,' where measurable disparities in AI system performance against these groups act as a proxy for detecting systemic bias against the broader disability category.
proposals.method: We will adapt Morris's expertise in long-term instrumentation (e.g., gaze typing) and agent-based simulation to create a multi-method auditing protocol. First, we will conduct a participatory design study with disability communities to identify candidate indicator subgroups and relevant performance metrics (e.g., image description quality for screen reader users, text generation appropriateness for AAC users). Second, we will develop and validate an auditing framework using these subgroups, testing it on pre-trained generative models (e.g., GPT-4, Stable Diffusion) and communication interfaces. The framework will employ both behavioral data from instrumented interactions and controlled agent-based simulations of user subgroups.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, pragmatic, and standardized methodology for disability bias auditing, moving beyond abstract categories to actionable, subgroup-focused metrics. It will produce a validated protocol and open-source tools for researchers and practitioners to assess AI systems, directly informing the design of more equitable generative and social computing technologies.
proposals.novelty: The core novelty is the conceptual translation of the ecological indicator species paradigm to algorithmic fairness, providing a principled, interdisciplinary approach to managing heterogeneity in bias auditing. This moves beyond common demographic fairness approaches by defining subgroups based on functional interaction with technology, not just identity labels.

proposals.title: Auditing for Ableism: Adapting Bias Frameworks from Gender and Race to Disability in Generative Language Models
proposals.motivation: Bias auditing for AI has focused heavily on attributes like gender and race, but disability remains critically understudied despite its profound impact on communication and social participation. Pre-2026 work in accessibility, such as analyzing image descriptions on Wikipedia, reveals systemic gaps, while the rise of generative AI introduces new risks of perpetuating stereotypes or excluding disabled users. This gap is urgent as generative models become integrated into assistive technologies and general communication platforms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that bias auditing frameworks developed for gender and race can be effectively adapted to quantify disability-related bias in generative language models, but that this adaptation will require novel operationalizations of disability concepts and reveal distinct bias patterns not captured by existing protected attributes.
proposals.method: We will adapt and extend pre-2026 bias auditing methods (e.g., log probability scoring, embedding coherence tests) to a new disability lexicon derived from disability studies literature and community guidelines. Using a multi-agent simulation framework, we will generate and evaluate text completions for prompts involving disability-related scenarios versus neutral counterparts. We will instrument this process with behavioral metrics and gaze data simulation (inspired by AAC research) to measure cognitive load and stereotype salience in the generated outputs across multiple model families (e.g., GPT-4, Llama 2).
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, publicly available auditing framework specifically for disability bias in generative AI. It will contribute empirical findings on the prevalence and forms of ableism in state-of-the-art language models, providing critical groundwork for more inclusive AI design and informing policy discussions around equitable AI.
proposals.novelty: While bias auditing is established, its systematic application to disability in generative AI is novel. The approach uniquely integrates Morris's expertise in communication instrumentation and generative AI with a rigorous transfer of bias measurement paradigms, moving beyond surface-level sentiment to measure representational harm and communicative exclusion.

proposals.title: Modeling AI Harms to Persons with Disabilities as an Ecological Risk: A Framework for Holistic Failure Mode Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current AI fairness and safety audits are often component-focused, failing to capture the complex, cascading failures that can occur when AI systems interact with the socio-technical ecosystems of Persons with Disabilities (PWD). As generative AI and agentic systems become integrated into assistive technologies and communication aids (e.g., gaze-typing systems), there is an urgent need for methodologies that systematically anticipate context-sensitive harms like loss of autonomy or social exclusion, which are well-documented concerns in pre-2026 HCI and accessibility literature.
proposals.hypothesis: Adapting the Ecological Risk Assessment (ERA) framework to model AI systems as stressors within the lived ecosystem of a PWD will yield a more comprehensive and actionable set of failure modes than traditional, isolated component analysis.
proposals.method: We will conduct a series of participatory design workshops with PWD who use AI-assisted communication technologies, informed by Morris's prior work on long-term instrumentation (e.g., SpeakFaster Observer). Using causal loop diagramming from system dynamics, we will co-construct conceptual models mapping AI stressors (e.g., unreliable generative AI features) to exposure pathways (e.g., dependency on a feature for social interaction) and adverse effects (e.g., social withdrawal). These models will be formalized into a structured taxonomy and validated via agent-based simulations to test the propagation of failures through the modeled ecosystem.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, structured framework for AI risk assessment centered on the ecosystem of the user, moving beyond model-centric audits. It will produce a publicly available taxonomy of AI-PWD failure modes and a methodological blueprint for applying ecological analogies to technology safety, directly informing more robust and equitable AI design for accessibility.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual translation of a well-established environmental science methodology (ERA) to the domain of AI harm analysis for PWD, shifting the unit of analysis from the AI system in isolation to its role within a dynamic human-technical ecosystem.

proposals.title: ContextCompass: A Meta-Framework for Dynamic Synthesis of Accessibility Standards in Generative AI Interaction
proposals.motivation: Current approaches to accessible AI design, such as applying the WCAG guidelines statically, struggle with the dynamic, multi-modal nature of generative AI (e.g., conversational agents, image generators). This creates a tension between universal principles and domain-specific effectiveness, as a guideline for physical spaces may be irrelevant for a text-based chatbot. A systematic, automated method to select and integrate the most relevant accessibility standards from diverse fields (architecture, software, education) based on real-time context is critically needed but absent.
proposals.hypothesis: An AI system equipped with a meta-framework that dynamically selects and synthesizes accessibility guidelines from a cross-domain evidence base, based on real-time analysis of user context and interaction modality, will produce more effectively accessible interactions than systems using a static, single-domain set of principles.
proposals.method: We will construct a knowledge graph of pre-2026 accessibility standards (WCAG, Universal Design for Learning, ADA, etc.), tagging each guideline with contextual metadata (domain, modality, user ability). A rule-based and retrieval-augmented agent will use this graph. For a given interaction (e.g., a user with low vision requesting an image description via voice), the agent will analyze the context (user profile from instrumentation like gaze, task, modality) using a classifier, retrieve and weight relevant guidelines, and synthesize a context-specific design prescription. We will prototype this within a slide-deck generation AI, evaluating via simulated agents and user studies with AAC users.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a formalized, computable meta-model for contextual accessibility, 2) an open, evidence-based library of cross-industry standards mapped to AI interaction parameters, and 3) empirical evidence on the efficacy of dynamic versus static guideline application for generative AI systems, advancing the field beyond checklist compliance.
proposals.novelty: While prior work catalogs accessibility issues (e.g., in Wikipedia images) or explores AI's potential, no framework exists to dynamically *orchestrate* standards from non-AI domains (architecture, transport) to inform real-time AI design decisions, making the accessibility adaptation itself an intelligent, generative process.

proposals.title: A Dual-Agent Framework for Systematic Mapping of AI's Assistive Benefits and Discriminatory Harms for People with Disabilities
proposals.motivation: While generative AI promises significant assistive benefits for people with disabilities (PWD), such as automated image description or communication aids, it also risks perpetuating and amplifying societal biases, leading to discriminatory outputs and inaccessible interfaces. Current research, including Morris's work on image accessibility and scientists' views on generative AI, often examines benefits or harms in isolation, lacking a unified methodological framework to systematically study both phenomena concurrently within the same interaction context.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a dual-agent simulation framework, where one agent embodies a PWD persona and another a generative AI system, can reveal a quantifiable tension between task completion efficacy (benefit) and the rate of biased or inaccessible interactions (harm), and that this tension varies predictably across disability contexts and AI model architectures.
proposals.method: We will develop a simulation environment using a multi-agent architecture (e.g., based on frameworks like Generative Agents). A 'User Agent' will be parameterized with specific disability profiles (e.g., visual impairment, motor speech disorder) influencing its interaction channel (inspired by gaze-typing instrumentation). A 'Generator Agent' will utilize contemporary large language and vision models (e.g., GPT-4, CLIP) to respond to requests. We will systematically run the paired agents through curated task scenarios (e.g., information seeking, content creation), logging both successful assistive outcomes and harmful events (e.g., biased text, inaccessible format). Analysis will employ behavioral logging and structured coding from prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel methodological tool for the concurrent study of AI benefit and harm, moving beyond anecdotal evidence. It will produce a foundational, structured map of risk-benefit trade-offs across disability contexts, providing actionable guidance for AI developers and policymakers focused on equitable design.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated, simulation-based approach that operationalizes the dual problem within a single experimental framework, leveraging agent-based modeling to study dynamic interaction patterns that static audits or single-case studies cannot capture.

ideas.title: Design culturally-adaptive social search systems

ideas.title: Contextual Disability Bias Auditing via Participatory Multi-Axis Ontologies
ideas.description: This research develops a bias auditing framework specifically for disability by first building a contextual ontology of disability through participatory design with disabled communities and experts. This ontology maps the multiple, intersecting dimensions relevant to algorithmic fairness, such as type of impairment, interaction with assistive technology, and environmental barriers. The auditing system then uses this structured framework to generate targeted tests for bias across specific disability-related contexts and intersections, rather than applying a one-size-fits-all transfer from other protected attributes. This ensures audits are conceptually sound, actionable, and relevant to the lived experiences of disabled people affected by automated systems.

ideas.title: AI for PWD is primarily assistive, not harmful

ideas.title: A Meta-Framework for Contextually-Adaptive Universal AI Design
ideas.description: This research proposes a meta-framework for AI system development that integrates the foundational philosophy of universal design with a dynamic, evidence-based library of cross-industry accessibility standards. Instead of applying a single, fixed set of principles, the AI system or its developers would use a governing meta-model to assess the user context, task domain, and interaction modality, then intelligently select and synthesize the most relevant and effective guidelines from domains like architecture, software, education, and transportation. This enables the creation of AI that is not just broadly accessible but optimally and efficiently accessible for specific scenarios, resolving the tension between universalism and domain-specific effectiveness by making the choice itself an automated, principled function of the system's design process.

ideas.title: A Framework for Post-Development Accessibility Integration via Cross-Domain Pattern Libraries
ideas.description: This research challenges the notion that effective accessibility must be foundational by proposing a framework for successful post-development integration. It does so by arguing that the optimal path for such integration is not through generic, cross-industry standards, but through a curated library of highly effective, domain-specific accessibility patterns. The core investigation examines how a solution proven in one domain (e.g., tactile feedback in automotive interfaces) can be systematically identified, validated, and adapted for retrofitting into a different domain (e.g., enterprise software). This creates a pragmatic bridge between the need for deep, contextual effectiveness and the reality of legacy systems, offering a strategic alternative to costly re-architecting.

ideas.title: A Modular, Core-Profile Approach to Accessibility Standards
ideas.description: Challenging the assumption that cross-industry standards are optimal for accessibility, this research explores if a hybrid model, inspired by solutions in bioinformatics, could be more effective. Bioinformatics faced a similar tension between universal data standards and the needs of specialized subfields (e.g., microarray data vs. sequencing data) and solved it by creating modular standards with a mandatory core profile and optional, community-developed extension profiles. Adapting this, the research idea is to design and test a hierarchical accessibility framework consisting of a minimal 'Core Accessibility Profile' (CAP) for fundamental principles, upon which industries can build specific 'Domain Extension Modules' (DEMs). The study would evaluate whether this structure reduces the friction of one-size-fits-all standards while preventing the siloing and incompatibility of purely fragmented guidelines, ultimately aiming for a more effective and adoptable ecosystem of accessibility practices.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Risk Assessment for AI-PWD Failure Mode Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts the methodology of Ecological Risk Assessment (ERA) from environmental science to systematically anticipate how AI can harm Persons with Disabilities (PWD). The analogous solution involves modeling ecosystems, stressors, exposure pathways, and adverse effects. The adapted research idea applies this by framing AI systems as potential stressors and the lived environment of a PWD—including assistive technologies, social systems, and physical spaces—as a complex ecosystem. Researchers would develop conceptual models to trace failure modes from AI system attributes through specific exposure pathways (e.g., dependency on an unreliable feature) to ultimate harms (e.g., loss of autonomy, social exclusion), enabling a more holistic and context-sensitive failure analysis than traditional component-focused methods.

ideas.title: Quantifying the Market and Innovation Returns of Ability-Based VR Design
ideas.description: This research proposes a longitudinal, mixed-methods study to track VR development projects that adopt ability-based interaction paradigms from their inception. It will quantify the direct market benefits (e.g., user base expansion, retention) and, crucially, measure the secondary innovation spillovers—such as novel interaction techniques, improved user experience for all users, and reduced need for future retrofitting—that arise from this foundational design approach. The goal is to produce a robust cost-benefit model that reframes inclusive VR design from an ethical add-on to a primary driver of technical and commercial advancement in the immersive computing industry.

ideas.title: The Accessibility Bill of Materials (A11yBOM): Enforcing Accessibility as a Versioned, Machine-Verifiable Dependency
ideas.description: This research proposes a paradigm shift where accessibility requirements are treated as explicit, versioned dependencies, similar to software libraries, documented in a machine-readable Accessibility Bill of Materials (A11yBOM). The A11yBOM is integrated into the development toolchain, where CI/CD pipelines automatically verify compliance and flag regressions, making accessibility failures as visible and blocking as a broken build. By quantifying the technical debt and remediation costs of non-compliance in real-time, the system directly alters developer and business incentives, making inclusive design the default, economically rational path rather than a costly post-development add-on.

ideas.title: Adapting Ecological Indicator Species Monitoring to Disability Bias Auditing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from conservation biology to the challenge of transferring bias auditing frameworks to disability status. In ecology, scientists monitor specific 'indicator species' to assess the health of an entire ecosystem, as tracking every species is impractical. The analogous solution involves developing standardized protocols to monitor these proxy species. Adapted to algorithmic bias auditing, this means identifying specific disability-related user subgroups (like screen reader users or individuals with a particular motor impairment) that can serve as measurable 'indicator subgroups.' By creating and validating auditing frameworks focused on these subgroups, researchers can establish proxies for detecting systemic bias against the broader, highly heterogeneous disability category. This approach provides a pragmatic and standardized method to audit for disability bias, mirroring how ecologists use targeted monitoring to infer the condition of a complex whole.

ideas.description: This research develops a formal specification framework that translates universal design principles into precise, machine-readable constraints for AI system behavior and outputs. It creates tools for developers to embed these specifications into model training (as constraints or objectives) and system deployment (as runtime monitors). The approach enables automated testing and verification against accessibility and fairness criteria, providing concrete evidence of compliance. This moves beyond advisory guidelines to create enforceable, auditable engineering standards for universally designed AI.

ideas.title: Applying a Modular, Layered Standardization Approach from Early Internet Protocols to VR
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that VR must either have no fixed standards or fully mature, monolithic standards. Drawing an analogy from the development of internet protocols during their own period of 'near-maturity' and competing models in the 1970s-80s, we adapt the solution of a modular, layered architecture. The analogous field is early computer networking, where the TCP/IP suite succeeded by standardizing a minimal, stable core (the internet layer) while allowing innovation in the layers above (transport, application) and below (link). Adapted to VR, this suggests a research agenda to define a 'VR protocol stack.' The work would involve identifying which aspects of VR (e.g., a universal coordinate system, basic user presence data format) are stable enough to form a fixed, core standard—the 'waist' of the stack. Concurrently, it would define open interfaces for higher, evolving layers like rendering pipelines, haptic feedback APIs, and social interaction protocols. This approach aims to provide the stability needed for widespread development and interoperability without locking the technology into premature or overly restrictive standards that could hinder its crucial final stages of maturation.

ideas.title: Ecosystem Services Valuation as a Model for Inclusive Design ROI
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that the cost-benefit of inclusive design is always positive for developers by drawing an analogy from environmental economics. In that field, the analogous problem of justifying conservation costs was solved by creating the 'ecosystem services' framework, which quantifies and monetizes indirect, long-term benefits provided by natural systems. Adapting this solution, the research would develop a novel valuation model for inclusive design. This model would move beyond simple cost vs. expanded market revenue analysis to systematically quantify indirect 'design ecosystem services,' such as innovation spillovers, brand equity enhancement, future regulatory resilience, and reduced lifecycle support burdens. By applying this adapted framework to case studies across software and hardware development, the research aims to provide a more rigorous, holistic method for determining when and why inclusive design yields a positive return on investment, thereby testing the core assumption with greater nuance.

ideas.title: Accessibility as a First-Principle Constraint: A Thermodynamic Analogy for System Design
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that accessibility can be effectively added post-development by drawing an analogy from thermodynamics and physical chemistry. In those fields, the degradation of energy (increase in entropy) is fundamentally irreversible; you cannot retrofit efficiency into a system designed without exergy conservation as a core principle. The analogous solution is to treat efficiency constraints as foundational, using exergy analysis from the start. Adapting this, the research proposes to model accessibility requirements as similar first-principle constraints in system design. It would develop a formal framework where inaccessibility is treated as a form of 'design entropy,' demonstrating quantitatively how early architectural choices generate irreversible barriers. The outcome is a method proving that foundational integration is not just preferable but necessary, as post-hoc fixes incur unsustainable costs and remain fundamentally incomplete, much like trying to recover useful work from dissipated heat.
